story:
As a child, Pixie-Bob could taste tremors on his tongue like stray hairs. He warned the adults about earthquakes and vast spaces under the city where wishes gathered and waited. The adults laughed. They said earthquakes don’t happen here. Anyway, they were busy in sunlit suburbs, drinking from silver spigots, standing on artificial grass, tossing barbecue scraps to anonymous hands that sometimes reached up from the ground.

Grown, Pixie-Bob moves to the edge of the East Village and makes his living photographing B-girls in the last hours of their swan dance. There he discovers the tunnels—entered through manholes, subway shafts, the cracks in your mother’s existence, or sometimes just by dropping a wish. Underground live the Undercats, girls with soot-dusted faces and Low Kitten dialect, who sit alone in the last car of the subway, one eye tilted upward, listening to a stolen iPod. They like Progressive Jazz with hard direction and Tokyopop. The air down there is poor; even kissing requires care.

Pixie-Bob falls in love with an Undercat named Mango Soff. She tells him how her family survived the 4th Ave. Wars on the handouts of rich Cover-Ups at barbecues. It is hard to breathe in a bubble, she says, but she would rather dream of light than be a Cover-Up with no sense of tempo. They share a night scored by a snatched track, and the next day Mango Soff is gone—not because the moment was too beautiful, but because the air and her fragile lungs take her.

Every day afterward, Pixie-Bob returns to the same spot in the tunnel where they met. Her body turns to dust and, in time, to a large bubble with a tiny tear. Breathless, he carries the bubble upstairs and sets it in a warm corner of his bedroom, beside old family photos posed in happy denials and careful cover-ups. He gets sentimental at the memory of artificial grass.

A girl breaks through the bubble. She grows up thin and anemic, haunted by the ghost of a woman’s voice and a craving for mangoes. Pixie-Bob buys the fruit at the 6th Ave market and names her Peach-Purr. To protect her from the harshness above ground, he keeps her inside, away from daylight that might weaken her.

Then the earthquake no one believed in arrives. The city shakes itself apart. Pixie-Bob’s building collapses. At the hospital, between bouts of half-sleep, he asks for his daughter. He is certain she won’t last long alone, with no mangoes and no one to count her breaths. One night he slips out in a green striped gown and slippers, growling at the stares of strangers.

Peach-Purr pulls herself from the rubble of their building. By Undercat instinct, she finds the exact tunnel where she was once bubbled. In the thick dark she hears footsteps and counts the stranger’s breaths. “It’s me,” says Pixie-Bob. “Your father.” She smiles; no one told her she is blind. They hold hands in the old air and pretend they’re listening to light jazz. The tunnel closes in. The breath they have left is small. In that thin music, father and daughter fade to stillness, together at last in the place where wishes form.
